sections.title: Topic Particles: 은/는
sections.explanation: Korean uses small words called particles (조사) that attach directly to nouns to show their grammatical role. The topic particles 은 and 는 mark the topic of a sentence — what you are talking about. Use 은 after a consonant-ending noun and 는 after a vowel-ending noun. For example: 저는 학생이에요 (As for me, I am a student). The topic particle emphasizes contrast or introduces the general topic. It is often compared to 'as for...' or 'speaking of...' in English. When comparing two things, 은/는 highlights the contrast: 사과는 좋아요, 바나나는 싫어요 (Apples I like, bananas I don't like). The topic particle is one of the most fundamental and frequently used particles in Korean.
sections.tip: After a consonant (받침), use 은. After a vowel, use 는. Example: 책 ends in ㄱ → 책은; 나 ends in ㅏ → 나는. Think of 은/는 as setting the stage — 'As for X, ...'

examples.en: I am a student. (는 after vowel 저)
Korea is in Asia. (은 after consonant 국)

options: 는 — because 책 ends in a vowel
이 — because it is a subject
은 — because 책 ends in a consonant (ㄱ)
exercises.explanation: 책 ends with the consonant ㄱ (batchim), so you attach 은: 책은. The rule is simple: 은 after consonants, 는 after vowels.

exercises.prompt: What does the topic particle 은/는 do in a sentence?
options: Marks the direct object of a verb
Marks the topic — what the sentence is about
Marks the location of an action
exercises.explanation: 은/는 marks the topic of the sentence. It tells the listener 'as for this thing, here is what I want to say about it.' It can also imply contrast with something else.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence: 커피___ 맛있어요. (Coffee is delicious.)
options: 는
를
은
exercises.explanation: 커피 ends with the vowel ㅣ, so the topic particle 는 is used: 커피는 맛있어요. Remember: 는 after vowels, 은 after consonants.

sections.title: Subject Particles: 이/가
sections.explanation: The subject particles 이 and 가 mark the grammatical subject — who or what performs an action or is being described. Use 이 after a consonant-ending noun and 가 after a vowel-ending noun. The key difference from 은/는: while 은/는 marks the general topic (old or known information), 이/가 highlights the specific subject (often new information or emphasis on identity). For example: 누가 왔어요? — 민수가 왔어요. (Who came? — Minsu came.) Here, 가 emphasizes that it is specifically Minsu. Compare: 민수는 학생이에요 (As for Minsu, he is a student — topic/general info) vs. 민수가 학생이에요 (Minsu is the student — identifying who the student is). Subject particles are also used with descriptive expressions: 날씨가 좋아요 (The weather is nice — as a new observation).
sections.tip: Use 이/가 when answering 'who?' or 'what?' questions or when presenting new information. Use 은/는 when talking about a known topic. After consonants: 이. After vowels: 가.

examples.en: The cat is cute. (가 after vowel 이)
The water is cold. (이 after consonant ㄹ)
Who did it? (가 marks the unknown subject)

exercises.prompt: Which particle follows 비 (rain) as a subject?
options: 는 — because 비 is the topic
이 — because 비 ends in a consonant
가 — because 비 ends in a vowel
exercises.explanation: 비 ends with the vowel ㅣ, so the subject particle 가 is used: 비가 와요 (It is raining / Rain comes). After vowels: 가. After consonants: 이.

exercises.prompt: Someone asks 누가 학생이에요? (Who is the student?) — How would you answer?
options: 민수가 학생이에요. (Minsu is [the one who is] a student.)
민수를 학생이에요.
민수는 학생이에요. (As for Minsu, he is a student.)
exercises.explanation: When answering a 'who?' question, you use 이/가 to identify the specific person: 민수가 학생이에요 — it is Minsu who is the student. 은/는 would shift the meaning to general information about Minsu.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 시간___ 없어요. (I have no time. / There is no time.)
options: 이
은
가
exercises.explanation: 시간 ends with the consonant ㄴ, so the subject particle 이 is used: 시간이 없어요. The subject particle marks 시간 as what is being described (it does not exist).

sections.title: Object Particles: 을/를
sections.explanation: The object particles 을 and 를 mark the direct object of a verb — the thing that receives the action. Use 을 after a consonant-ending noun and 를 after a vowel-ending noun. For example: 밥을 먹어요 (I eat rice) — 밥 is what is being eaten. 커피를 마셔요 (I drink coffee) — 커피 is what is being drunk. Korean sentence order is typically Subject-Object-Verb (SOV): 저는 책을 읽어요 (I read a book — literally 'I, book, read'). The object particle is essential for clarity because Korean word order can be flexible; the particles, not position, tell you each noun's role. In casual speech, particles are sometimes dropped, but using them correctly is important for clear and polite communication.
sections.tip: After a consonant (받침), use 을. After a vowel, use 를. Always remember Korean word order is SOV: Subject + Object + Verb. The verb always comes last!

examples.en: I eat rice. (을 after consonant ㅂ)
I drink coffee. (를 after vowel ㅣ)
I watch a movie. (를 after vowel ㅏ)

exercises.prompt: Which particle completes this sentence? 음악___ 들어요. (I listen to music.)
options: 을 — because 악 ends in a consonant (ㄱ)
이 — because 음악 is the subject
를 — because 악 ends in a vowel
exercises.explanation: 음악 ends with the consonant ㄱ, so the object particle 을 is used: 음악을 들어요 (I listen to music). 을 after consonants, 를 after vowels.

exercises.prompt: What is the typical word order in a Korean sentence?
options: Verb-Subject-Object (VSO)
Subject-Object-Verb (SOV)
Subject-Verb-Object (SVO)
exercises.explanation: Korean follows Subject-Object-Verb order. The verb always comes at the end: 저는 (I) 책을 (book) 읽어요 (read) — literally 'I book read.' This is different from English SVO order.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct sentence for 'I study Korean.'
options: 저는 한국어를 공부해요.
저는 한국어가 공부해요.
저는 한국어을 공부해요.
exercises.explanation: 한국어 ends with the vowel ㅓ, so the object particle 를 is used: 한국어를 공부해요. Using 을 would be incorrect because 을 follows consonants only.
